summary: Julia Evans shares a collection of small personal programming projects she's written to solve minor problems in her life, from scraping festival calendars to generating envelope labels. She frames these tiny projects not as practical problem-solving (many could have been solved faster without code) but as a joyful way to spend an evening or weekend programming. The projects range from API polling for vaccine appointments to reverse engineering file formats, all united by being low-stakes, fun, and personally useful. She recommends this approach especially for people learning to program, emphasizing that the best part is the combination of creative fun and tangible real-world output.
key_points: Tiny personal programs that solve minor life problems are a great way to have fun with programming, even when they're not the most practical solution
Many of her projects involve discovering and using undocumented APIs by copying cookies from the browser — a recurring pattern she enjoys
The best tiny projects share three qualities: they're just for you (low stakes), finishable in an evening or weekend, and produce tangible output
Several projects turned out to not be useful in the end, but the process of building them was the real value
These low-stakes personal projects are especially valuable for people learning to program
money_quotes: I like to write small programs that have some marginal utility in my life.
Many of the problems I've solved with programming could have been solved in less time in other ways.
As long as your goal is actually to have fun programming and your programs don't hurt anyone else, I think this is a great approach.
One of my favourite things is to use secret undocumented APIs where you need to copy your cookies out of the browser to get access to them.
I don't think this actually helped us at all with buying a condo but it was fun.
They're just for me (if it goes wrong, it doesn't matter!)
I can finish them in an evening or weekend (it's not a Huge Giant Thing hanging over my head).
Presumably you could calculate the same thing with math, but it's easier with code.
key_insight: The most rewarding personal programming projects are low-stakes, completable in a single session, and solve some small real problem in your life — even if the solution isn't strictly necessary.
